text: Chrysippus, the third head of the Stoic school, argued that emotions are not feelings that happen to us but judgments we make. Fear, for instance, is not a sensation but the judgment that something bad is about to occur. This cognitive theory of emotion has a direct implication for attention: if emotions are judgments, then catching the moment of judgment — before it consolidates — is enough to interrupt the emotion. Prosoche becomes not merely a contemplative exercise but a form of real-time cognitive intervention. You do not wait until anger has flared; you notice the micro-judgment that would produce anger and examine whether it is warranted before granting it authority.
question: What implication does Chrysippus's cognitive theory of emotion have for prosoche?

options.text: It means emotions must be suppressed through rational willpower before they are felt
It makes prosoche a form of real-time cognitive intervention by catching judgments before they consolidate into emotion
It shifts attention away from impressions toward the physical sensations that accompany judgment
It implies that emotions are outside the domain of rational control entirely
